extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain hair during mourning
verbatim: And alle wommen that wole not lette schaven hem, thei payen this penance, that upon o day thei profren hem for achat of here beautee; but the merkat is open to straungers alle only, and the huyr becometh an offring to Venus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Lucian describes a general ritual law in Byblos where women are faced with a choice during the mourning of Adonis: they must either shave their heads or undergo a day of ritual prostitution with foreigners to donate the earnings to the temple. This indicates that women do not have an unconditional right to keep their hair during this period.

extracted_rules.rule: Female exclusion from hair ritual earnings
extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain one's own earnings
verbatim: And alle wommen that wole not lette schaven hem, thei payen this penance, that upon o day thei profren hem for achat of here beautee; but the merkat is open to straungers alle only, and the huyr becometh an offring to Venus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text specifies that the money earned by women who choose ritual prostitution over head-shaving is not kept by the women themselves, but must be surrendered as an offering to the goddess Venus (Astarte), showing a lack of control over their specific labor/ritual earnings.

verbatim: Thei leven fissches holy thynge, and thei ne touchen fissche never; and though of othere foules thei eten alle, the dowve thei ne eten not, but sche is holy, as thei wenen. And thise thinges ben don, thei trowen, be cause of Derketoun and Semiramys, the oon for that Derketoun hath schap of a fissche, and the tother because that atte laste Semiramys tornede to a dowve.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Lucian describes a contemporary religious taboo in the Holy City where the inhabitants (Syrians) are forbidden from eating fish because they consider them holy, linked to the goddess Derceto.

verbatim: Thei leven fissches holy thynge, and thei ne touchen fissche never; and though of othere foules thei eten alle, the dowve thei ne eten not, but sche is holy, as thei wenen. And thise thinges ben don, thei trowen, be cause of Derketoun and Semiramys, the oon for that Derketoun hath schap of a fissche, and the tother because that atte laste Semiramys tornede to a dowve.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Similar to the fish taboo, the text explicitly states that the local population does not eat doves, viewing them as holy due to the legend of Semiramis.

verbatim: Also he spak of Galles that ben in the temple, seyinge that Galles gelden hem and counterfeten Attis not at alle for no worschipe of luno but for worschipe of Cibella.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific group of temple servants (Galli) who undergo castration as a requirement or defining rite of their status and service in the temple, imitating the myth of Attis.

verbatim: Twyes eech yeer water cometh fro the See in to the temple. And not prestes only bryngen it, but al Surrye and Arabye; and fro beyonden Eufrate gon manye men to the See and bryngen alle watre, that anon thei scheden out in the temple, and thanne it goth adoun in to that hole; and al be it that the hole is smal, natheles it taketh inne gret plentee of water.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a general practice where the broader population of Syria and Arabia (not just the priests) is expected to travel to the sea to bring water for a specific biannual ritual at the temple.

extracted_rules.rule: Father surrender of wife to son
verbatim: Stratonice was daughter of Demetrius Poliorcetes and wife of Seleucus Nicator; she was subsequently surrendered by him to his son Antiochus I, Soter, by a former wife, Apama.

Than was the fader overcomen, and yold bothe wyf and rewme (realm) to his sone, and goyinge himself to the contree of Babyloyne leet make a cytee nyghe Eufrate that was cleped after his owne name, ther as his dethe befel.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific historical custom or precedent where a father (Seleucus) transfers his wife to his son to preserve the son's life/health, indicating wives could be treated as transferable assets within the royal lineage.

verbatim: “For thou art noble, Daun Combabe, I love thee most of alle mine frendes, and I preyse thee gretli for thy coninge and for thy gode wille to me, that thou hast discovered beforn. And now me nedeth of grete feyth, wherfore I wole that thou folwe my wyf, for to acomplisshe the werke in my name, and to perfourme the sacrifises, and to reule the hoost; and whan thou retornest thou schalt gete highe worschipe fro me.”
extracted_rules.reasoning: The King selects Combabe for a high-status mission (building the temple and ruling the host) specifically citing his noble status as the primary qualification for such a role of trust.

verbatim: And now me nedeth of grete feyth, wherfore I wole that thou folwe my wyf, for to acomplisshe the werke in my name, and to perfourme the sacrifises, and to reule the hoost; and whan thou retornest thou schalt gete highe worschipe fro me.”

Therwith anon Combabe gan preye and beseche him ful besily that he scholde not send him forth ne betaken (entrust) him nouther that tresor, that was moche to en:rust gret for him, ne his wyf, ne the holy werk.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Even though the Queen is the one who received the divine command to build the temple, she is not permitted to lead the project or the host alone; a male noble (Combabe) must be appointed to oversee the work and the sacrifices in the King's name.

verbatim: wommen coveyten Galles and Galles wexen wode for love of wommen; natheles is no man ialous, but hem thenketh this thing right holy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social rule in the Holy City where the interactions between women and Galli (eunuchs) are exempt from the standard social jealousy that would otherwise apply to male-female interactions.

verbatim: And thou shalt come before me withouten that ony man presente thee, and noon schalle lette thee fro sight of me, thoghe I be abedde with my wyf.
extracted_rules.reasoning: As a reward for his loyalty, the King grants Combabe the rare legal privilege of entering the royal presence at any time without being announced or barred, even in the King's private quarters.

verbatim: And thise no lenger clothen hem as men, but weren wommenes wedes and don wommenes werkes. And as I herde, the blame of this also is leyde on Combabe; for a thyng befel him in this wyse. . . Thanne for despeyr that Combabe hadde be cause he was acurst in love, he didde on femele clothinge to the ende that never non other womman scholde ben so begyled. That is whi Galles weren femele aparayl.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific ecclesiastical group (the Galli) who, as a rule of their Order, are prohibited from wearing male attire and must perform female tasks, imitating the founder Combabus.

extracted_rules.resource: Right of entry to the temple
verbatim: Of Combabe have I seyd ynow, and of Galles I schalle make mencioun sone in another partie of my boke, how that they ben gelded, and in what manere that thei ben buryed, and wherfore thei entren not into the temple.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly identifies a rule or condition whereby the Galli are restricted from entering the temple, which he intends to explain later.

extracted_rules.rule: Priestly access to the inner chamber
verbatim: In to the grete temple comen alle men, but in to the litylle chambre the prestes only, and not alle the prestes, but only thei that ben most nyghe to the Goddes and han in governaunce alle the servys of the temple.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This establishes a clear hierarchy of access to sacred spaces. While the general temple is open to all men, the inner chamber is restricted to a specific elite subset of the priesthood.

extracted_rules.resource: Right of entry to the temple
verbatim: In to the grete temple comen alle men, but in to the litylle chambre the prestes only, and not alle the prestes, but only thei that ben most nyghe to the Goddes and han in governaunce alle the servys of the temple.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text specifies that 'alle men' have the right to enter the main temple area, implying a positive right of access based on gender/status.

verbatim: And everyche yeer a newe chefe preste is sett over hem, that allone wereth a robe of purpre and is crouned with a coronale of gold.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific rank (the chief priest) within the religious hierarchy of Hieropolis who possesses the exclusive right to wear purple and a gold crown, distinguishing him from the 300 white-robed priests.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to wear a golden crown
verbatim: And everyche yeer a newe chefe preste is sett over hem, that allone wereth a robe of purpre and is crouned with a coronale of gold.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The chief priest is distinguished from other priests by the exclusive right to wear a gold crown during his year of office.

verbatim: And manye Galles and tho religious men that I spak of pertourmen here cerimonyes; and thei kutten here owne armes and beten that oon that other upon the bak. . . . This is don withouten the temple, and thei that don it comen not in to the temple.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a general rule during festivals where the Galli (eunuch priests) perform their rituals outside because they are barred from entering the temple building itself.

verbatim: And that house into the whiche he schalle casten thise [his genitals], he getteth thens femele wedes and wommanlyche aparayles. Thus don thei whan thei gelden hem.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Upon initiation through self-emasculation, the social rule dictates that the individual transitions to a group (Galli) that has the right/obligation to wear female clothing provided by the community.

verbatim: And Galles at here dyenge ben not enterred in lyk manere as other men, but gif a Galle dye, his felawes liften him up and carryen him in to the skirtes of the Cytee and sette doun the man himself and the fertre on the whiche thei broghte him, and easten stones aboven; and whan this is don, thei wenden hoom ayen.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific and different burial rite for Galli compared to 'other men,' involving being left on the outskirts and covered with stones rather than standard interment.

verbatim: And thei wayten for the nombre of 7 dayes or that thei entren in to the temple; for if thei entren before, thei misdon.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the death of one of their members, the surviving Galli are subject to a mandatory 7-day purification period during which they are prohibited from entering the temple.

verbatim: If so be that ony of hem seeth a dede man, he cometh not in to the temple that day; but on the nexte daye, aftre that he hath pured him, thanne he entreth. And tho that ben of the dede mannes kyn wayten for the space of 30 dayes and lette schaven here hedes or thei entren; but before that this hath ben don, it is not leful for to entren.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Lucian describes a general religious prohibition in the Holy City where contact with death or being a relative of the deceased bars an individual from entering the temple for a specific period of purification.

verbatim: Thei sacrificen boles and kyn and gotes and schepe. Swyn only thei ne sacrificen not nouther eten be cause that thei demen hem unclene.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a general dietary and sacrificial restriction based on religious purity laws within this society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hunt or touch doves
verbatim: And amonges briddes the dowve semeth hem wondur holy thing, and thei ben not wont so moche as to touchen hem; and gif thei touchen hem maugree hem selven, thei ben unclene that day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Doves are afforded a special holy status that prevents the population from touching or harming them, with a penalty of religious impurity if they do.

verbatim: he useth cold watre bothe for to wasschen with and to drynken, and slepeth alle weyes on the erthe; for he ne may not liggen in no maner bedde un to tyme that his pilgrimage be fulfilled and he be comen ayen to his owne contree.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Individuals in the state of pilgrimage are bound by ascetic rules that deny them the use of beds until their return home.

extracted_rules.group: Specific lineages in the Holy City
extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as pilgrim hosts
verbatim: For certeyne men in that place ben apoynted unto everyche cytee as hostes, and dyverse kynredes han this office of linage.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The role of 'host' for visiting pilgrims is an inherited office belonging to specific families or lineages.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to keep hair before marriage
verbatim: Men of Trosen han made ordeynaunce as touchinge the maydens and the bachelers, that thei schulle not maryen or thei lette scheren here lokkes for worschipe of Ypolite; and so thei don. That thing is don also in the Holy Cytee. The bacheleres offren of here berdes, and the children from here birthe leten holy crulles growe, the which thei scheren whan thei ben presented in the temple
extracted_rules.reasoning: There is a mandatory religious rule for young people to shear and dedicate their hair/beards in the temple before they are permitted to marry.
